concept.title: Cell Structure & Organelles
body: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life. All living organisms are composed of one or more cells.
Mitochondria are membrane-bound organelles that generate most of the cell's ATP through cellular respiration, earning them the nickname the powerhouse of the cell.

Plant cells differ from animal cells in three key ways: they have a rigid cell wall (made of cellulose) for structural support, chloroplasts for photosynthesis, and a large central vacuole for storage and maintaining turgor pressure.

The nucleus houses the cell's genetic material (DNA) and controls gene expression and cell activities. The endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi apparatus work together to process, modify, and package proteins.
Ribosomes, found free in the cytoplasm or attached to the rough ER, are the sites of protein synthesis (translation).

questions.question: Which organelle is known as the 'powerhouse of the cell'?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria produce ATP via cellular respiration (oxidative phosphorylation), earning the nickname 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: Which of the following are found in a plant cell but NOT in an animal cell? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Plant cells have cell walls (cellulose), chloroplasts (photosynthesis), and a large central vacuole. Animal cells have mitochondria too.

questions.question: A typical human cell undergoing aerobic respiration produces approximately how many ATP molecules per glucose molecule? (Enter the commonly cited textbook value)
questions.explanation: The commonly cited textbook value for ATP yield per glucose in aerobic respiration is approximately 36-38 ATP (some modern estimates say ~30-32, but 38 is the classic figure).

concept.title: Cell Division — Mitosis
body: Mitosis is the process by which a single cell divides to produce two genetically identical daughter cells, essential for growth and tissue repair.
Prophase: chromatin condenses into visible chromosomes; the nuclear envelope begins to break down; spindle fibers start forming.
Metaphase: chromosomes align at the cell's equatorial plate (metaphase plate), attached to spindle fibers via centromeres.
Anaphase: sister chromatids separate as centromeres split, and they are pulled to opposite poles of the cell by spindle fibers.
Telophase: chromosomes decondense, nuclear envelopes reform around each set of chromosomes, and the cell proceeds to cytokinesis (division of the cytoplasm).

questions.question: During which phase of mitosis do chromosomes align at the cell's equatorial plate?
questions.explanation: During Metaphase, chromosomes align at the metaphase plate (cell equator) and are attached to spindle fibers.

questions.question: Which of the following events occur during Anaphase? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: During Anaphase: sister chromatids separate, centromeres split, and chromatids move to opposite poles. Nuclear envelope reforms in Telophase.

questions.question: A human somatic cell has 46 chromosomes before mitosis. How many chromosomes will each daughter cell have after mitosis is complete?
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells, each with the same number of chromosomes (46) as the parent cell.

concept.title: Mendelian Genetics
body: Gregor Mendel's experiments with pea plants established the fundamental laws of inheritance.
The Law of Segregation states that each organism carries two alleles for each gene, which separate during gamete formation so each gamete carries only one allele.

In a monohybrid cross between two heterozygotes (Aa × Aa), the genotypic ratio of offspring is 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa, while the phenotypic ratio (assuming complete dominance) is 3 dominant : 1 recessive.
A Punnett square is a diagram used to predict the genotypes and phenotypes of offspring from a genetic cross.
Dominant alleles mask the expression of recessive alleles when both are present (heterozygous), while recessive traits only appear when an individual is homozygous recessive.

questions.question: In a monohybrid cross between two heterozygotes (Aa × Aa), what is the phenotypic ratio of offspring?
questions.explanation: Aa × Aa produces AA:Aa:aa = 1:2:1 genotypic ratio, but 3 dominant (AA+Aa) : 1 recessive (aa) phenotypic ratio.

questions.question: In a dihybrid cross (AaBb × AaBb), what is the total number of distinct phenotype combinations possible (assuming complete dominance for both genes)?
questions.explanation: A dihybrid cross with complete dominance produces a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio — i.e., 4 distinct phenotype classes.
